deepdive.history_jp_en: Chogakuji Temple is an ancient temple of the Koyasan Shingon sect set along the Yamanobenomichi (Mountain Border Road) at 508 Yanagimoto-cho, Tenri City, Nara Prefecture. According to the Chogakuji official source and the Nara Prefecture Tourism official source, it is said to have been founded in the Tencho 1 (824 CE) by Kobo Daishi Kukai as the jingū-ji (shrine-temple) of Yamato Jinja (Omiwa Shrine), by imperial vow of Emperor Junna. The principal object of worship is a triad of Amida Nyorai (Important Cultural Property), and the precinct contains medieval to early modern structures including the main hall, main gate, bell tower, and kitchen — one of the ancient temples of the Yamato region. Each year, for approximately 40 days from October 23 to November 30, a nine-scroll Hell and Paradise painting (jigoku-goku-e) is specially displayed. According to the Chogakuji official source and the Nara Prefecture Tourism official source, this painting is said to be an Edo-period work in a nine-scroll, single-suite composition, depicting the Ten Kings Tribunal (Juokai, the ten-stage judgment in which the Ten Kings judge the deceased's accumulated sins and merits during the 49 days after death), the River of Three Crossings (Sanzu no Kawa), the Old Hag Who Strips the Robes (Datsueba), the Eight Great Hells (Hacchi daijigoku: the eight types of hell including Toto, Kokujo, Shugo, Kyokan, Dakyokan, Shonetsu, Daishonetsu, and Muken), and Paradise (Gokuraku Jodo) — visualizing the rokudō thought (six realms), views of hell, and views of the Pure Land. During the period, the head priest delivers a picture-explanation sermon (etoki) centered on around 13:00 on weekends and holidays, and pilgrims can view all nine scrolls one by one together with the head priest's sermon — directly experiencing how people before the early modern era visualized hell, paradise, and karmic retribution, and how they used this as guidance for living. [Chogakuji Official: Commentary on Chogakuji's Hell and Paradise Painting](https://www.chogakuji.or.jp/chogakujiN/%E8%A7%A3%E8%AA%AC%EF%BC%9A%E9%95%B7%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA%E3%81%AE%E6%A5%B5%E6%A5%BD%E5%9C%B0%E7%8D%84%E7%B5%B5/), [Nara Prefecture Tourism Official: Chogakuji](https://inori.nara-kankou.or.jp/inori/hihou/chogakuji/event/twavcn8y4f/)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Hell paintings and Paradise paintings were produced and displayed at temples throughout Japan from the Heian to Edo periods, forming the core of an "etoki" (picture-explanation) culture in which the Buddhist views of the afterlife — rokudō thought (six realms: hell, hungry ghosts, animals, fighting spirits, humans, heaven), the Ten Kings veneration (a Chinese and Taoist-derived view of the afterlife in which ten kings judge the deceased), and Pure Land Buddhism (rebirth in Amitabha's Paradise) — were visualized in paintings to convey teachings of karmic retribution, good and evil deeds, and rebirth in the Pure Land even to those with low literacy. Remnants of hell and paradise paintings have been passed down nationally, including the Yamagoe Amida-zu at Konkaikomyo-ji (Kyoto), hell paintings at Zenkoji (Nagano), hell paintings at Kogawa-dera (Wakayama), and the Ten Kings scrolls at Rokuharamitsuji (Kyoto), but the Chogakuji Dai Jigoku-e is a rare example in which both the large-scale nine-scroll composition and the etoki sermon — a form of oral religious tradition from the early modern period — are still being transmitted in a face-to-face manner today. This painting display is not merely "cultural property public display" but a living etoki venue transmitting the oral religious culture of the etoki sermon in a face-to-face context to the present day, and it is a research subject for folklore, religious studies, art history, and medieval Japanese Buddhist history. The autumn season display (October 23–November 30) falls in the period from after the Higan (equinox) to the autumn foliage period, forming a religious space-time in which Buddhist views of the afterlife — memorialization of the deceased, ancestor veneration, rebirth in the Pure Land — overlap with the seasonal transition (autumn leaves = the end of life).
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Yanagimoto-cho, Tenri City, is an ancient settlement along the Yamanobenomichi (said to be one of Japan's oldest roads), where ancient Yamato royal authority-related burial mounds and shrines are scattered, including Yamato Jinja (Omiwa Shrine), the mausolea of Emperor Sujin and Emperor Keiko, Kurozuka Kofun, and others — it is the central zone of the ancient history of the Yamato region. Chogakuji has deep ties to Yamato Jinja, where it was founded as its jingū-ji, retaining a strong coloring of the history of shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), and is locally affectionately known as "the Kobo Daishi-san of Kama-no-kuchi." The Dai Jigoku-e Kaichō (Grand Hell Painting Display) is an annual observance for local parishioners, ujiko (parishioners), and pilgrims during the autumn equinox to the autumn foliage season, and is transmitted in partnership with the network of ancient temples along the Yamanobenomichi (e.g., the Yamato Shichifuku-hassho Meguri circuit). For hikers and ancient-temple pilgrims on the Yamanobenomichi, the autumn pilgrimage experience combining autumn foliage, hell paintings, and the etoki sermon is remembered as one of the most impressive experiences in ancient Yamato temple pilgrimage. Viewers are strongly expected to approach the hell paintings with religious respect and the etoki sermon with a posture of quiet participation. [Chogakuji Official](https://www.chogakuji.or.jp/chogakujiN/%E8%A7%A3%E8%AA%AC%EF%BC%9A%E9%95%B7%E5%B2%B3%E5%AF%BA%E3%81%AE%E6%A5%B5%E6%A5%BD%E5%9C%B0%E7%8D%84%E7%B5%B5/), [Nara Prefecture Tourism Official: Chogakuji](https://inori.nara-kankou.or.jp/inori/hihou/chogakuji/event/twavcn8y4f/)

deepdive.best_visit_time_en: The Dai Jigoku-e Kaichō period runs for approximately 40 days each year from October 23 to November 30. As the head priest's etoki sermon is delivered centered on around 13:00 on weekends and holidays during the period, visiting on weekends and holidays is recommended for those wishing to attend, though crowds and wait times are expected. Weekdays allow for relatively quiet viewing. Peak autumn foliage is mid to late November, and the pilgrimage experience combining hell paintings and paradise paintings with autumn foliage is a highlight of ancient Yamato temple pilgrimage. Prepare clothing and water for both the cool mornings and evenings and strong midday sun in autumn.
deepdive.photo_tips_en: The precinct, main hall, temple buildings, and autumn foliage can often be photographed, but photography inside the main hall and in front of the hell paintings is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on the day regarding photography and flash permissions. Strictly refrain from photography or recording during the etoki sermon so as not to disturb the head priest's or pilgrims' concentration. Prioritize respect for the privacy and movements of pilgrims and etoki sermon participants within the precinct, and do not publish compositions with people in frame without consent. The combination of autumn foliage and the main hall and main gate is a composition that symbolizes Chogakuji in autumn, offering different expressions in the soft morning light and afternoon side-light.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- Chogakuji is an ancient Koyasan Shingon sect temple founded by Kobo Daishi Kukai as the jingū-ji of Yamato Jinja in Tencho 1 (824 CE), according to tradition.
- The Dai Jigoku-e is said to be an Edo-period work in a nine-scroll, single-suite composition.
- Depicts rokudō thought visualized: the Ten Kings Tribunal, River of Three Crossings, Datsueba, Eight Great Hells, and Paradise.
- Display period is approximately 40 days each year from October 23 to November 30.
- Head priest delivers the etoki sermon centered on around 13:00 on weekends and holidays during the period.
- An ancient temple along the Yamanobenomichi, locally affectionately known as "the Kobo Daishi-san of Kama-no-kuchi."
- The principal object of worship is an Amida Nyorai Triad (Important Cultural Property).
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: Chogakuji is an ancient temple along the Yamanobenomichi, and the precinct, main hall, and kitchen contain steps and narrow passages; the pilgrimage path includes many sections of stone paving and gravel, so visiting in easy-to-walk shoes is recommended. The Dai Jigoku-e Kaichō period (October 23–November 30) overlaps with the autumn foliage season, and weekends and holidays and etoki sermon days are crowded with pilgrims; confirm viewing positions, visiting hours, and whether the etoki sermon is being given by checking the official source before visiting. The hell paintings are objects of religious reverence, precious early modern Buddhist paintings depicting the Ten Kings, Datsueba, Eight Great Hells, and Paradise; mocking, prurient, or curiosity-seeking viewing is strictly to be avoided, and respect for religion, pilgrims, and the head priest must take precedence. Photography inside the main hall and in front of the hell paintings is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography permissions and worship protocol. Strictly refrain from photography or recording during the etoki sermon so as not to disturb the head priest's and pilgrims' concentration. Prepare against the cool mornings and evenings and strong sun during the autumn foliage season (warm clothing, hat, water).

summary: 山形県鶴岡市大網（旧朝日村）、湯殿山の里宮口に位置する真言宗智山派の古刹・注連寺（ちゅうれんじ）に祀られる即身仏・鉄門海上人（てつもんかいしょうにん、1759-1829）を拝観できる出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院の一つ。注連寺公式・鶴岡市観光連盟の案内によれば、鉄門海上人は江戸後期の湯殿山の行者で、自らの片眼を奉納し木食行（穀絶ち・五穀十穀断ち）・荒行・千日修行を経て、衆生救済の願をもって土中入定（生きながら土中に埋まり念仏を唱えながら入定）した僧として、現在も即身仏として注連寺本堂に安置され、一般拝観が可能である。本寺は森敦『月山』の舞台としても知られ、雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹に湯殿山信仰の極致と日本近代文学が重なる、出羽三山・庄内即身仏巡礼の代表的な目的地の一つ。
highlights: 江戸後期に土中入定した即身仏・鉄門海上人（1759-1829）を本堂に安置
出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院、真言宗智山派の古刹・注連寺
森敦『月山』の舞台としても知られる雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹
from_kameyama: 車で約9時間（東名阪→新名神→名神→北陸道→日本海東北道→山形道経由）／新幹線＋特急いなほ＋バスで約7時間（亀山→名古屋→東京→新潟→鶴岡→大網方面）
category_label: 即身仏・湯殿山信仰・出羽三山・民俗信仰・庄内即身仏巡礼
safety_note: 即身仏は宗教的崇敬の対象であり、見世物的・怪奇的・興味本位の観覧は厳に慎み、参拝者・寺院・地域信徒への敬意を最優先する。本堂内は撮影禁止または制限される場合が多く、撮影可否・拝観作法は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。注連寺は不定休・要事前確認の運営で、団体対応不可・予約推奨など条件があるため、訪問前に必ず公式・鶴岡市観光連盟で拝観時間・拝観料・予約要否・道路状況を確認する。庄内地方の山間立地のため、冬季（12〜4月）は積雪・路面凍結・道路閉鎖が常態で、雪装備（スタッドレス・チェーン）と防寒着が必須。冬季は受付時間が短縮または閉所される場合がある。木食行・土中入定という極限的修行の歴史を伝える施設であり、信仰・宗教文化への理解と尊厳ある観覧姿勢が求められる。隣接する大日坊（真如海上人）と合わせて庄内即身仏巡礼として参詣する場合は、両寺の拝観時間・予約状況を事前調整する。

deepdive.history_jp: 注連寺は、山形県鶴岡市大網字中台92-1、湯殿山の里宮口にあたる庄内の山あいに位置する真言宗智山派の古刹で、注連寺公式・鶴岡市観光連盟の案内によれば、弘法大師空海が湯殿山を開いた際に湯殿山参詣の里宮・行所として開創したと伝わる出羽三山・湯殿山信仰の中核寺院の一つである。本寺に祀られる即身仏・鉄門海上人（てつもんかいしょうにん、1759-1829）は江戸後期の湯殿山の行者で、注連寺公式・鶴岡市観光連盟の案内によれば、若き日に幕府役人との争いから自らの片眼を奉納し、木食行（穀絶ち・五穀十穀断ち）・荒行・千日修行を経て肉身を浄化したのち、衆生救済の願をもって土中入定（生きながら土中に埋まり念仏を唱えながら入定）したとされ、その後一定期間を経て掘り出され即身仏として本堂に安置された。鉄門海上人は江戸・湯島聖天や江戸庶民への布教活動でも知られ、眼病平癒・衆生救済の祈願対象として広く信仰を集めた。現在も即身仏として注連寺本堂に安置され、一般拝観が可能である。本寺はまた、戦後の作家・森敦が長期滞在し執筆した芥川賞受賞作『月山』（1973年）の舞台としても知られ、雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹に湯殿山信仰の極致と日本近代文学が重なる、独特の宗教・文化空間を形成している。[注連寺公式](https://www.shimekake.jp/)、[鶴岡市観光連盟](https://www.tsuruokakanko.com/spot/261)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 即身仏（即身成仏、ミイラ仏とも俗称される）は、湯殿山信仰圏（山形県庄内地方）を中心に北日本に数体現存する、木食行・荒行・千日修行を経て土中入定し肉身を遺した行者の遺骸を、後年掘り出して本堂に安置・拝観対象とする独特の宗教文化遺産である。鉄門海上人（注連寺）・真如海上人（大日坊）・本明海上人（本明寺）・忠海上人（注連寺）・円明海上人（注連寺）・妙心法師（横蔵寺）・弘智法印（西生寺）など、現存する即身仏は全国で16体程度とされ、その大半が湯殿山信仰圏に集中する。本信仰は、湯殿山の御神体（湯が湧き出る赤茶色の巨岩）への秘儀的崇拝と、真言密教・修験道・浄土教・民間信仰が混淆した独自の山岳宗教文化を背景に成立しており、行者が自らの身体を究極の供物として衆生救済に捧げるという日本仏教史上稀有な実践として、民俗学・宗教学・仏教史・医学史の研究対象でもある。注連寺は鉄門海上人を中心に複数の即身仏・湯殿山信仰関連史料を擁し、隣接する大日坊（真如海上人）と合わせて庄内即身仏巡礼の中核を成す。森敦『月山』が描いた注連寺の閉ざされた冬の宗教空間は、日本近代文学における山岳宗教・即身仏文化の代表的表象として、文学史・宗教文化史の双方から論じられてきた。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 鶴岡市大網（旧朝日村）は、出羽三山・湯殿山の里宮口にあたる庄内の山あいの集落で、冬は積雪2〜3mに達する豪雪地帯である。注連寺・大日坊は地元では湯殿山参詣の起点・即身仏巡礼の中核寺院として古くから信仰を集め、地域住民・檀家・氏子・湯殿山修験者・全国の参詣者によって護持されてきた。地元では即身仏を「センセイ」「シンニョカイサマ」「テツモンカイサマ」などの敬称で呼び、宗教的崇敬の対象として日常的な祈願・先祖供養の場として位置づけている。観光化された見世物的視点を排し、湯殿山信仰・木食行・土中入定の宗教的核心と地域共同体の継承努力への敬意ある参詣姿勢が強く期待される。冬季は積雪・路面凍結・道路閉鎖の可能性が高く、注連寺自体も不定休・受付時間短縮となるため、訪問前の公式情報確認が必須である。森敦『月山』を読んでから訪問することで、雪深い庄内の山あいの古刹が持つ宗教・文学空間がより立体的に体験できる、特異な参詣地である。[注連寺公式](https://www.shimekake.jp/)、[鶴岡市観光連盟](https://www.tsuruokakanko.com/spot/261)

deepdive.photo_tips: 本堂内・即身仏前は撮影禁止または制限される場合が多く、撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。境内・山門・参道・周辺の山並み・庄内の雪景色は撮影可能な範囲が多いが、見世物的・怪奇的・興味本位の構図は厳に慎み、宗教・参詣者・地域信徒への敬意を最優先する。森敦『月山』の文学的記憶と重ねた庄内山あいの古刹の佇まいは、四季ごとに異なる表情を見せる。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Shimekake-ji (formally: Chūsan Shimekake-ji) is an ancient Shingon Chisan-branch temple located at 92 Nakadai, Oami, Tsuruoka City, Yamagata Prefecture, in a mountain valley of the Shonai region at the entrance of the Yudono-san pilgrimage. According to the Shimekake-ji official source and the Tsuruoka City Tourism Association, the temple is said to have been founded as the satomi-guchi (village pilgrimage entrance) and practice site when Kobo Daishi Kukai opened Yudono-san, making it one of the central temples of the Three Mountains of Dewa / Yudono-san veneration. The sokushinbutsu (immediate-body Buddha) enshrined in this temple, Tetsumon-kai Shonin (1759–1829), was an ascetic practitioner of Yudono-san in the late Edo period. According to the Shimekake-ji official source and the Tsuruoka City Tourism Association, in his youth, during a dispute with a government official, he offered his own eye as a pledge and made a vow; after undergoing mokujiki-gyo (grain abstinence), severe asceticism, and a thousand-day training regimen to purify his body, he is said to have entered soil-entry samadhi (underground, chanting nembutsu) with the vow to save all sentient beings; subsequently, after a period of time, he was exhumed and enshrined as a sokushinbutsu in the main hall. Tetsumon-kai Shonin was also known for his missionary activities among the people of Edo (present-day Tokyo), including at Yushima Tenjin (Yushima Seiten), and attracted widespread veneration as an object of prayer for eye disease healing and the salvation of all sentient beings. Even today he is enshrined as a sokushinbutsu in the main hall of Shimekake-ji, open for public veneration. This temple is also known as the setting for the Akutagawa Prize-winning novel "Gassan" (Moon Mountain, 1973) by postwar author Mori Atsushi, who resided there for an extended period while writing it, forming a distinctive religious and cultural space in which the extreme of Yudono-san veneration and Japanese modern literature overlap in a snow-deep mountain temple of the Shonai region. [Shimekake-ji Official](https://www.shimekake.jp/), [Tsuruoka City Tourism Association](https://www.tsuruokakanko.com/spot/261)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Sokushinbutsu (also popularly called mummy Buddha) are a unique religious cultural heritage found in small numbers mainly in northern Japan (centered on the Shonai region of Yamagata Prefecture) — the remains of ascetic practitioners who, after undergoing mokujiki-gyo, severe asceticism, and a thousand-day training regimen, entered soil-entry samadhi and left their bodies, which were subsequently exhumed and enshrined as objects of veneration in the main hall. Currently existing sokushinbutsu include Tetsumon-kai Shonin (Shimekake-ji), Shinnyo-kai Shonin (Dainichi-bo), Homei Shonin (Homei-ji), Chukai Shonin (Komukai-ji), Enmeikai Shonin (Komukai-ji), Myoshin Hoshi (Yokura-ji), and Kochi Hoin (Saiseiji), and approximately 16 are said to exist nationwide, with the majority concentrated in the Yudono-san veneration sphere. This faith was established against the background of the unique mountain religion culture in which the shintai (sacred object) of Yudono-san (a reddish-brown giant boulder from which hot water springs forth) receives secret veneration, and Shingon Esoteric Buddhism, Shugendo, Pure Land Buddhism, and folk belief are fused — with practitioners offering their bodies as the ultimate votive offering to save all sentient beings, a practice unique in Japanese Buddhist history, and also a research subject for folklore, religious studies, Buddhist history, and medical history. Shimekake-ji is home to Tetsumon-kai Shonin and multiple Yudono-san veneration-related historical materials, and together with the adjacent Dainichi-bo (Shinnyo-kai Shonin), forms the core of the Shonai sokushinbutsu pilgrimage. The literary memory of Mori Atsushi's "Gassan" depicting Shimekake-ji's closed-off winter religious space has been discussed from both literary history and religious culture history perspectives as a representative cultural expression of mountain religion and sokushinbutsu culture in modern Japanese literature.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Oami, Tsuruoka City (formerly Asahi Village), is a mountain settlement in the Shonai region at the satomi-guchi (village pilgrimage entrance) of the Three Mountains of Dewa / Yudono-san, a heavy-snowfall area where winter snow accumulation can reach 2–3 meters. Shimekake-ji and Dainichi-bo have long attracted veneration locally as the starting point of Yudono-san pilgrimage and the central temples of sokushinbutsu pilgrimage, and have been maintained by local residents, parishioners, ujiko (parishioners), Yudono-san ascetic practitioners, and pilgrims from across Japan. Locally, sokushinbutsu are respectfully referred to as honorifics such as "Sensei," "Shinnyo-kai-sama," or "Tetsumon-kai-sama," and are positioned as everyday sites of prayer and ancestor veneration — objects of religious reverence. Excluding a tourism-oriented sideshow perspective, and a respectful posture toward the Yudono-san veneration and mokujiki-gyo and soil-entry samadhi religious core and the community's transmission efforts is strongly expected. Winter brings a high possibility of snow accumulation, road icing, and road closures; Shimekake-ji itself may also have irregular closures and shortened reception hours, so confirming official information before visiting is essential. Reading Mori Atsushi's "Gassan" before visiting allows the religious and literary space of the ancient Shonai mountain valley temple to be experienced more three-dimensionally — a unique pilgrimage site. [Shimekake-ji Official](https://www.shimekake.jp/), [Tsuruoka City Tourism Association](https://www.tsuruokakanko.com/spot/261)

deepdive.best_visit_time_en: From after the snow melt in spring to autumn (May–October), both roads and visiting hours are most stable. Fresh greenery (May–June) and autumn foliage (mid to late October) are the most beautiful seasons in the Shonai mountain valley. Shimekake-ji operates on irregular days; advance confirmation is recommended, with conditions such as no groups and reservations preferred, so always confirm visiting hours, admission fees, and reservation requirements with the official source and the Tsuruoka City Tourism Association before visiting. In winter (December–April), there is a high possibility of snow accumulation, road icing, and road closures; Shimekake-ji itself may be closed or operating with shortened reception hours. It is most advisable to visit the adjacent Dainichi-bo (Shinnyo-kai Shonin) together as part of the Shonai sokushinbutsu pilgrimage over a full day.
deepdive.photo_tips_en: Photography inside the main hall and in front of the sokushinbutsu is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography permissions. The precinct, mountain gate, approach path, surrounding mountains, and Shonai snow scenery are generally photographable, but strictly refrain from sideshow-style, sensational, or prurient compositions, and respect for religion, pilgrims, and local faithful must take priority. The ambience of the ancient Shonai mountain valley temple, resonant with the literary memory of Mori Atsushi's "Gassan," offers different expressions in each of the four seasons.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- Shimekake-ji is said to have been founded as the satomi-guchi (village pilgrimage entrance) and practice site when Kobo Daishi Kukai opened Yudono-san — an ancient Shingon Chisan-branch temple.
- Tetsumon-kai Shonin (1759–1829) was a late-Edo-period Yudono-san ascetic practitioner also known for having offered his own eye as a pledge.
- Underwent mokujiki-gyo, severe asceticism, and thousand-day training before entering soil-entry samadhi; subsequently exhumed and enshrined as a sokushinbutsu in the main hall.
- Known for missionary activities in Edo including at Yushima Seiten; attracted veneration as an object of prayer for eye disease healing and the salvation of all sentient beings.
- Shimekake-ji carries multiple Yudono-san veneration-related historical materials centered on Tetsumon-kai Shonin.
- Setting of Mori Atsushi's "Gassan" (1973, Akutagawa Prize).
- Together with the adjacent Dainichi-bo (Shinnyo-kai Shonin), forms the core of the Shonai sokushinbutsu pilgrimage.
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: Sokushinbutsu are objects of religious reverence; sideshow-style, sensational, or prurient viewing is strictly to be avoided, and respect for worshippers, the temple, and local faithful must take precedence. Photography inside the main hall is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography permissions and worship protocol. Shimekake-ji operates on irregular days with advance confirmation recommended, with conditions such as no groups and reservations preferred, so always confirm visiting hours, admission fees, reservation requirements, and road conditions with the official source and the Tsuruoka City Tourism Association before visiting. As a mountain location in the Shonai region, snow accumulation, road icing, and road closures are the norm in winter (December–April); snow equipment (studded tires/chains) and warm clothing are essential; reception hours may be shortened or the temple may be closed. As a facility that transmits the history of the extreme practice of mokujiki-gyo and soil-entry samadhi, an attitude of understanding and dignity toward faith and religious culture is required. When visiting together with the adjacent Dainichi-bo (Shinnyo-kai Shonin) as part of the Shonai sokushinbutsu pilgrimage, coordinate in advance the visiting hours and reservation status of both temples.

hours: 公式サイト掲載の拝観時間に準ずる（地獄堂の閻魔像・地獄の釜・極楽度地獄度チェックは公開時間内のみ要確認）
summary: 大阪市平野区平野本町、大阪市内最古級の聖徳太子開創伝承を持つ高野山真言宗の古刹・全興寺の境内に併設された、地獄表現と六道思想を体験的に見せる小堂「地獄堂」を中核とする民間信仰・地獄観のテーマ拠点。全興寺公式の案内によれば、地獄堂内には等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆（だつえば）・地獄の鬼などが安置され、参拝者は「地獄の釜の音」を聞く装置、極楽度・地獄度を判定するチェックボックス、六道輪廻を体感する仏間など、近世以来の地獄・極楽観を現代的に翻案した体験装置で六道思想に触れることができる。境内には「ほとけのくに（仏の国）」「駄菓子屋さん博物館」など多彩な小展示も併設され、寺院資料館とは異なる庶民信仰と地獄表現の体験装置として、大阪の隠れたB級スポット・寺院体験施設の代表格として親しまれてきた。
highlights: 等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆・地獄の鬼を安置する体験型の地獄堂
「地獄の釜の音」装置・極楽度地獄度チェック・六道輪廻体感装置を備える
聖徳太子開創伝承を持つ大阪市内最古級の高野山真言宗古刹・全興寺の境内施設
from_kameyama: 車で約2時間（東名阪→新名神→近畿道→阪神高速・松原線経由）／JR・近鉄・地下鉄で約2時間30分（亀山→名古屋→新大阪→大阪→地下鉄谷町線平野駅徒歩約5分）
category_label: 地獄表現・閻魔・六道思想・体験型寺院・庶民信仰・大阪平野郷
safety_note: 全興寺は大阪市平野区の住宅地・商店街に位置する街中の古刹で、境内・地獄堂・各小展示は狭隘・段差を含むため、歩きやすい靴での参拝が望ましい。地獄堂内の閻魔像・奪衣婆・地獄の鬼の表現は近世以来の地獄観に基づく宗教的造形物であり、子ども向けの単なるホラーアトラクションではなく、六道思想・因果応報・浄土往生を伝える庶民信仰の体験装置として捉える必要がある。揶揄的・興味本位の観覧は厳に慎み、参拝者・寺院・地域住民への敬意を最優先する。地獄堂内の撮影可否・撮影マナー・拝観作法は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。住宅地の中の寺院であり、参道周辺の地域住民の生活・通行を妨げない動線で参拝する。平野郷（大阪市平野区）は中世以来の自治都市の歴史を持つ寺町・商店街地区で、自転車・通行人が多いため、寺院前の道路・路地での歩行・停車には十分注意する。

deepdive.history_jp: 全興寺は、大阪府大阪市平野区平野本町4-12-21に位置する高野山真言宗の古刹で、全興寺公式の案内によれば、寺伝では聖徳太子が薬師如来像を彫り当地に小堂を建てたことを開創とし、大阪市内最古級の歴史を持つと伝えられる平野郷（中世以来の自治都市・寺町）の中核寺院の一つである。本尊は薬師如来で、地域では「平野のお薬師さん」として親しまれてきた。本寺の境内には、近世以来の地獄・極楽観・六道思想を体験的に見せる小堂「地獄堂」が併設され、等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆（だつえば）・地獄の鬼などが安置される。全興寺公式の案内によれば、地獄堂内では「地獄の釜の音」を聞く装置、極楽度・地獄度を判定するチェックボックス、六道輪廻を体感する仏間など、近世以来の地獄・極楽観を現代的に翻案した体験装置が設置されており、参拝者は六道思想・因果応報・浄土往生という近世以前の日本人の来世観・宗教観に身体的に触れることができる。境内には「ほとけのくに（仏の国）」「駄菓子屋さん博物館」など多彩な小展示も併設され、寺院資料館とは異なる庶民信仰と地獄表現の体験装置として、大阪の隠れたB級スポット・寺院体験施設の代表格として親しまれてきた。[全興寺公式](https://www.senkouji.net)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 地獄堂・地獄絵・閻魔像・奪衣婆像は、平安〜江戸期にかけて日本各地の寺院で制作・安置され、六道思想（地獄・餓鬼・畜生・修羅・人間・天）・十王信仰（中国・道教由来の十王が亡者を裁く来世観）・浄土教（阿弥陀如来による極楽浄土往生）などの仏教来世観を可視化することで、識字率の低い庶民にも因果応報・善悪業報・浄土往生の教えを伝える「絵解き」「視覚教化」文化の中核を成してきた。京都・六波羅蜜寺の十王像、奈良・長岳寺の大地獄絵、千葉・延命寺の地獄絵、和歌山・粉河寺の地獄絵など、全国に地獄表現の遺品が伝わるが、全興寺の地獄堂は江戸〜近代の閻魔・奪衣婆像の保存・継承と並んで、「地獄の釜の音」「極楽度地獄度チェック」など現代的な体験装置を併用する稀有な事例である。これは単なるテーマパーク化ではなく、近世以来の絵解き文化を現代の参拝者にも届けるための寺院側の工夫であり、庶民信仰と地獄表現の現代的継承を示す民俗学・宗教社会学・地域文化史の研究対象でもある。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 平野郷（大阪市平野区平野本町）は、中世以来の自治都市・寺町として知られる地区で、坂上田村麻呂の子・坂上広野の荘園に由来すると伝わる古い町並みを残し、全興寺・大念仏寺・杭全神社・含翠堂など多くの寺社・歴史遺構が点在する。全興寺は地元では「平野のお薬師さん」「地獄堂のお寺」として親しまれ、子どもの社会科見学・寺院体験・夏休みの地獄堂見学などで世代を超えた地域住民・近隣住民が訪れる地域共同体の宗教・文化拠点である。地獄堂・「ほとけのくに」「駄菓子屋さん博物館」などの小展示は、寺院と地域住民・商店街・大阪市平野区の連携で運営され、平野郷の町並み保存・地域コミュニティ活性化の一翼を担っている。観覧者には地獄表現・閻魔信仰への宗教的敬意と、地域共同体の継承努力への尊重ある参拝姿勢が期待される。[全興寺公式](https://www.senkouji.net)

deepdive.best_visit_time: 通年参拝可能で、地獄堂・各小展示は公式案内の公開時間内であれば随時見学できる。土日祝・夏休み期間は子ども連れの見学客が多く、平日は比較的静謐に見学できる。お盆（8月中旬）・お彼岸（春秋）・年末年始などの仏教年中行事の時期は参拝者が増加し、寺院本来の宗教活動の場としての姿が見られる時期でもある。大阪市平野区の街中の古刹であり、地下鉄谷町線平野駅から徒歩約5分の好アクセスで、平野郷の他の寺社（大念仏寺・杭全神社）と組み合わせた寺町散策に向く。
deepdive.photo_tips: 境内・参道・本堂・地獄堂外観などは撮影可能な範囲が多いが、地獄堂内の閻魔像・奪衣婆像・地獄の鬼などの安置物は宗教的崇敬の対象であり、撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。地獄表現を揶揄的・ホラー目的・興味本位に撮影・拡散することは厳に慎み、近世以来の六道思想・因果応報の宗教文化への敬意を最優先する。参拝者・子ども連れ見学客のプライバシーに配慮し、人物が映り込む構図は同意なく公開しない。境内は街中の狭い空間であり、他の参拝者の動線を妨げない位置から撮影する。
deepdive.trivia:
- 寺伝では聖徳太子が薬師如来像を彫り開創したと伝わる大阪市内最古級の高野山真言宗古刹。
- 地獄堂には等身大の閻魔大王像・奪衣婆・地獄の鬼が安置される。
- 「地獄の釜の音」装置・極楽度地獄度チェック・六道輪廻体感装置などの体験装置を備える。
- 境内に「ほとけのくに（仏の国）」「駄菓子屋さん博物館」などの小展示も併設。
- 平野郷（中世以来の自治都市・寺町）の中核寺院の一つ。
- 地元では「平野のお薬師さん」「地獄堂のお寺」として親しまれる。

deepdive.warnings_extra: 地獄堂内の閻魔像・奪衣婆・地獄の鬼の表現は近世以来の地獄観に基づく宗教的造形物であり、子ども向けの単なるホラーアトラクションではなく、六道思想・因果応報・浄土往生を伝える庶民信仰の体験装置として捉える必要がある。揶揄的・興味本位の観覧・SNS拡散は厳に慎み、参拝者・寺院・地域住民への敬意を最優先する。地獄堂内の撮影可否・撮影マナー・拝観作法は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。住宅地の中の寺院であり、参道周辺の地域住民の生活・通行を妨げない動線で参拝する。平野郷は中世以来の自治都市の寺町・商店街地区で、自転車・通行人が多いため、寺院前の道路・路地での歩行・停車には十分注意する。境内・地獄堂・各小展示は狭隘・段差を含むため、歩きやすい靴での参拝が望ましい。最新の拝観時間・拝観料・公開条件は全興寺公式で必ず確認する。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Zenkooji Temple is an ancient Koyasan Shingon sect temple located at 4-12-21 Hirano Honmachi, Hirano Ward, Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture. According to the Zenkooji official source, temple tradition holds that Prince Shotoku carved a Yakushi Nyorai (Medicine Buddha) image and built a small hall on the site, making it one of the oldest temples in Osaka City and one of the central temples of Hirano-go (a self-governing town and temple town since the medieval period). The principal object of worship is Yakushi Nyorai, and it is locally affectionately known as "the Medicine Buddha of Hirano" ("Hirano no Oyakushi-san"). In the temple precinct, a small hall called the "Jigoku-do" (Hell Hall) has been established alongside the main hall, presenting the folk views of hell, paradise, and the six realms from the early modern period in an experiential manner, with enshrined life-size statues of Enma Daio (Great King Yama), Datsueba (the Old Hag Who Strips the Robes), and demons of hell. According to the Zenkooji official source, the Jigoku-do is equipped with experiential devices including a "Jigoku no Kama no Oto" (Sound of the Cauldrons of Hell) apparatus, a checklist for rating one's "paradise score" and "hell score," and a room for experiencing the rokudō (six realms) cycle — modern adaptations of early modern views of hell and paradise that allow pilgrims to physically engage with the early modern Japanese views of the afterlife: rokudō thought, karmic retribution, and rebirth in the Pure Land. The precinct also features various small exhibitions including "Hotoke no Kuni (Land of the Buddha)" and a "Dagashi-ya-san Hakubutsukan" (Penny-Candy Shop Museum), making it popular as a representative of Osaka's hidden B-kyū supotto and temple experience facilities, distinct from a religious archive museum. [Zenkooji Official](https://www.senkouji.net)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Hell halls, hell paintings, Enma statues, and Datsueba statues were produced and enshrined at temples throughout Japan from the Heian to Edo periods, forming the core of an "etoki" (picture-explanation) and "visual catechesis" culture in which Buddhist views of the afterlife — rokudō thought (six realms: hell, hungry ghosts, animals, fighting spirits, humans, heaven), Ten Kings veneration (a Chinese and Taoist-derived view of the afterlife in which ten kings judge the deceased), and Pure Land Buddhism (rebirth in Amitabha's Paradise) — were visualized to convey teachings of karmic retribution, good and evil deeds, and rebirth in the Pure Land even to those with low literacy. Remnants of hell expressions have been passed down nationally, including Ten Kings statues at Rokuharamitsuji (Kyoto), the Dai Jigoku-e at Chogakuji (Nara), hell paintings at Enmeiji (Chiba), and hell paintings at Kogawa-dera (Wakayama), but Zenkooji's Jigoku-do is a rare case that combines the preservation and transmission of Edo to modern-era Enma and Datsueba statues with modern experiential devices such as the "Cauldrons of Hell sound" and "paradise/hell score check." This is not mere theme-park-ification but represents the temple's ingenuity in conveying the etoki culture since the early modern period to modern pilgrims, and is a research subject for folklore, religious sociology, and regional cultural history as an example of the modern transmission of folk belief and hell expressions.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Hirano-go (Hirano Honmachi, Hirano Ward, Osaka City) is a district known as a self-governing town and temple town since the medieval period, retaining an old streetscape said to derive from the manor of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's son Sakanoue Hirono, with Zenkooji, Dai-nembutsuji Temple, Kumata Jinja, and Kansuido scattered among many other temples, shrines, and historical structures. Zenkooji is locally affectionately known as "the Medicine Buddha of Hirano" and "the Hell Hall temple," and serves as a community religious and cultural hub visited across generations by local residents and nearby inhabitants for children's social studies visits, temple experiences, and summer vacation hell hall visits. The Jigoku-do, "Hotoke no Kuni," and the Dagashi-ya-san Hakubutsukan small exhibitions are operated through a partnership between the temple, local residents, commercial district associations, and Osaka City's Hirano Ward, playing a role in the preservation of Hirano-go's streetscape and community vitalization. Viewers are expected to approach hell expressions and Enma veneration with religious respect and a reverent posture of participation in the community's transmission efforts. [Zenkooji Official](https://www.senkouji.net)
deepdive.related_works_en:
- Zenkooji Official Website
- Regional history research on Hirano-go self-governing town, temple town, and streetscape preservation
- Buddhist history, folklore, and religious sociology research literature on rokudō thought, Ten Kings veneration, Enma veneration, and Datsueba veneration
- Osaka City Hirano Ward regional cultural materials on history and culture
- Art history and religious studies research on hell paintings, hell expressions, and etoki culture
deepdive.external_reviews_en: [Zenkooji Official](https://www.senkouji.net)
deepdive.best_visit_time_en: Worship is possible year-round, and the Jigoku-do and various small exhibitions can be viewed at any time within the official public visiting hours. Weekends, holidays, and summer vacation periods are busy with families and children's visitors, while weekdays are relatively quieter for viewing. During Buddhist annual observances such as Obon (mid-August), Ohigan (spring and autumn equinoxes), and New Year, there are more pilgrims and the temple's original religious activities can be observed. As an ancient temple in an Osaka City urban neighborhood, access is excellent — approximately 5 minutes on foot from Tanikamachi Line Hirano Station — making it suitable for a temple town stroll combined with other temples and shrines in Hirano-go (Dai-nembutsuji, Kumata Jinja).
deepdive.photo_tips_en: The precinct, approach path, main hall, and Jigoku-do exterior can often be photographed, but enshrined objects inside the Jigoku-do such as the Enma statue, Datsueba statue, and demons of hell are objects of religious reverence; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography and flash permissions. Strictly refrain from photographing or sharing hell expressions in a mocking, horror-purpose, or prurient manner, and prioritize respect for the early modern rokudō thought and karmic retribution religious culture. Be mindful of the privacy of pilgrims and children's groups, and do not publish compositions with people in frame without consent. The temple is in a small space in an urban area; photograph from positions that do not obstruct other pilgrims' movements.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- An ancient Koyasan Shingon sect temple said to be one of the oldest in Osaka City, founded according to tradition when Prince Shotoku carved a Yakushi Nyorai image.
- The Jigoku-do enshrines life-size statues of Enma Daio, Datsueba, and demons of hell.
- Equipped with experiential devices including a "Jigoku no Kama no Oto" (Sound of the Cauldrons of Hell) apparatus, a paradise/hell score check, and a rokudō cycle experience device.
- Small exhibitions including "Hotoke no Kuni (Land of the Buddha)" and a "Dagashi-ya-san Hakubutsukan" are also in the precinct.
- One of the central temples of Hirano-go (a self-governing town and temple town since the medieval period).
- Locally affectionately known as "the Medicine Buddha of Hirano" and "the Hell Hall temple."
- Approximately 5 minutes on foot from Tanikamachi Line Hirano Station.
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: The Enma statues, Datsueba, and demons of hell in the Jigoku-do are religious sculptures based on early modern views of hell, and are not a children's horror attraction but must be understood as experiential devices of folk belief conveying rokudō thought, karmic retribution, and rebirth in the Pure Land. Strictly refrain from mocking, prurient, or entertainment-seeking viewing and social media sharing, and prioritize respect for pilgrims, the temple, and local residents. Follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography permissions, photography etiquette, and worship protocol inside the Jigoku-do; do not follow guidance that is not permitted. The temple is in a residential urban neighborhood; keep the approach path and surrounding area free for local residents' life and passage. Hirano-go is a medieval self-governing town with many bicycles and pedestrians; take full care of walking and stopping on roads and lanes in front of the temple. The precinct, Jigoku-do, and small exhibitions may contain steps and narrow passages; visiting in easy-to-walk shoes is recommended. Always confirm the latest visiting hours, admission fees, and public conditions with the Zenkooji official source.

name: 照円寺 地獄極楽絵図ご開帳
name_kana: しょうえんじ じごくごくらくえずごかいちょう
address: 〒920-0851 石川県金沢市笠市町1-20
status: 現存・春秋彼岸期に地獄極楽絵図特別公開
fee: 公式サイト掲載の拝観料に準ずる（特別公開期間中の拝観料設定あり・要事前確認）
hours: 公式サイト掲載の拝観時間に準ずる（地獄極楽絵図ご開帳は春・秋の彼岸期間中の特別公開・要事前確認）
summary: 石川県金沢市笠市町、金沢駅にほど近い浄土真宗本願寺派の寺院・照円寺で、毎年春と秋の彼岸期に行われる「地獄極楽絵図ご開帳」を中心とする寺宝公開行事。照円寺公式・石川県観光公式の案内によれば、本寺には江戸時代より伝わる地獄極楽絵図一具が伝来し、春・秋の彼岸の特別公開期間中に本堂内で参拝者に披露される。絵図には八種の地獄、六道輪廻、十種の極楽浄土などが描かれ、近世以前の日本人が地獄・極楽・因果応報をどう視覚化してきたかを一堂に見ることができる。金沢駅近接の市街地寺院でありながら、王道観光ルート（金沢城・兼六園・東茶屋街・近江町市場）から外れた、六道思想・地獄絵の背景を持つ短期公開型の文化財公開行事として、奈良・長岳寺の大地獄絵開帳などと並ぶ、近世仏教来世観の現代的継承を示す稀有な事例。
highlights: 江戸時代より伝わる地獄極楽絵図一具を春秋の彼岸期に本堂で特別公開
八種の地獄・六道輪廻・十種の極楽浄土を一堂に描く近世仏教来世観の絵画化
金沢駅近接の都市部寺院での短期公開型寺宝公開行事
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間30分（東名阪→新名神→名神→北陸道→金沢西IC経由）／新幹線で約3時間（亀山→名古屋→米原・敦賀経由・北陸新幹線金沢駅徒歩約10分）
category_label: 地獄絵・極楽絵・六道思想・浄土真宗・彼岸特別公開・金沢駅近接
safety_note: 照円寺は金沢駅近くの市街地寺院で、境内・本堂は段差・狭隘部を含み、参拝路は石畳・砂利道を含む場合があるため、歩きやすい靴での参詣が望ましい。地獄極楽絵図ご開帳は春・秋の彼岸期間中の短期特別公開で、毎年の正式日程・公開時間・予約要否は変動するため、訪問前に照円寺公式・石川県観光公式で必ず確認する。本絵図は宗教的崇敬の対象で、近世の貴重な仏画であり、揶揄的・興味本位の観覧は厳に慎み、宗教・参詣者・住職への敬意を最優先する。本堂内・絵図前は撮影禁止または制限される場合が多く、撮影可否・拝観作法は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。本寺には専用駐車場がないため、公共交通（JR・北陸新幹線金沢駅徒歩約10分）の利用が望ましく、自家用車利用時は近隣のコインパーキング利用となる。金沢市街地は冬季（12〜3月）に積雪が多く、特別公開が春彼岸に重なる場合は残雪・路面凍結への備えが必要。

deepdive.history_jp: 照円寺は、石川県金沢市笠市町1-20、JR・北陸新幹線金沢駅から徒歩約10分の市街地に位置する浄土真宗本願寺派の寺院で、照円寺公式・石川県観光公式の案内によれば、本寺には江戸時代より伝わる地獄極楽絵図一具が伝来し、毎年春と秋の彼岸期に行われる「地獄極楽絵図ご開帳」の特別公開期間中に本堂内で参拝者に披露される。絵図には八種の地獄（八大地獄＝等活・黒縄・衆合・叫喚・大叫喚・焦熱・大焦熱・無間）、六道輪廻（地獄・餓鬼・畜生・修羅・人間・天）、十種の極楽浄土などが描かれ、近世以前の日本人が地獄・極楽・因果応報・浄土往生をどう視覚化してきたかを一堂に見ることができる。本絵図は江戸期に成立した近世仏教来世観の絵画化として民俗学・美術史・宗教学的にも貴重な遺品で、彼岸期の年中行事「地獄極楽絵図ご開帳」として地域住民・参詣者に公開される伝統が照円寺で継承されてきた。金沢駅近接の市街地寺院でありながら、王道観光ルート（金沢城・兼六園・東茶屋街・近江町市場）から外れた位置にあり、六道思想・地獄絵の背景を持つ短期公開型の文化財公開行事として、奈良・長岳寺の大地獄絵開帳などと並ぶ、近世仏教来世観の現代的継承を示す稀有な事例である。[照円寺公式](https://syouenji.jp)、[石川県観光公式 ほっと石川旅ねっと](https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/spot/detail_22503.html)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 地獄絵・極楽絵は、平安〜江戸期にかけて日本各地の寺院で制作・公開され、六道思想・十王信仰・浄土教などの仏教来世観を絵画化することで、識字率の低い庶民にも因果応報・善悪業報・浄土往生の教えを伝える「絵解き」文化の中核を成してきた。京都・金戒光明寺の山越阿弥陀図、長野・善光寺の地獄絵、和歌山・粉河寺の地獄絵、京都・六波羅蜜寺の十王図、奈良・長岳寺の大地獄絵（九幅一具）など、全国に地獄絵・極楽絵の遺品が伝わるが、照円寺の地獄極楽絵図は八種の地獄・六道・十種の極楽浄土を一具で描く構成と、春秋の彼岸という仏教年中行事に合わせた短期特別公開という公開形式に特色がある。彼岸（春彼岸＝春分前後、秋彼岸＝秋分前後）は浄土往生・先祖供養と結びついた仏教の重要な年中行事で、彼岸期に地獄極楽絵図を公開する所作は、参詣者に来世観・因果応報・浄土往生を直接的に体感させる宗教教化の機能を果たしてきた。浄土真宗の寺院での地獄極楽絵図公開は、阿弥陀信仰・他力本願の文脈で行われ、地獄・極楽の対比を通じて阿弥陀如来の救済力と浄土往生の意義を伝える役割を担う。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 金沢市笠市町は、JR・北陸新幹線金沢駅西口側の市街地に位置し、近世以来の浄土真宗・本願寺派の寺院が集まる地区の一角を成す。照円寺は地元では金沢駅近接の市街地寺院として、近隣住民・檀家・遠方参詣者から親しまれてきた寺院で、春秋の彼岸期の地獄極楽絵図ご開帳は地域住民・檀家・参詣者の年中行事として継承されている。金沢の王道観光（金沢城・兼六園・東茶屋街・近江町市場・21世紀美術館）から外れた位置にあるため、観光客の自由な訪問より、地域住民・宗教関係者・地獄絵・六道思想に関心を持つ参詣者の特別公開期間中の訪問が中心となる。観覧者には地獄絵への宗教的敬意と、彼岸期の宗教年中行事への静謐な参加姿勢が強く期待される。[照円寺公式](https://syouenji.jp)、[石川県観光公式 ほっと石川旅ねっと](https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/spot/detail_22503.html)

deepdive.best_visit_time: 毎年春彼岸（春分前後）と秋彼岸（秋分前後）の特別公開期間中に地獄極楽絵図ご開帳が行われる。2026年の正式日程・公開時間・予約要否は変動するため、訪問前に照円寺公式・石川県観光公式で必ず確認する。特別公開期間中は参詣者で混雑する場合があり、平日午前中の比較的静謐な時間帯の参詣が望ましい。春彼岸は3月中下旬で金沢市街地は残雪が残ることもあり、防寒・防水靴の準備が必要。秋彼岸は9月中下旬で比較的過ごしやすい気候。
deepdive.photo_tips: 境内・本堂外観は撮影可能な範囲が多いが、本堂内・地獄極楽絵図前は撮影禁止または制限される場合が多く、撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。絵図を揶揄的・ホラー目的・興味本位に撮影・拡散することは厳に慎み、近世以来の六道思想・因果応報・浄土往生の宗教文化への敬意を最優先する。参詣者・絵図公開の様子を撮影する際は他参詣者のプライバシーに配慮し、人物が映り込む構図は同意なく公開しない。市街地の寺院であり、寺院周辺の地域住民・通行人の動線を妨げない位置から撮影する。
deepdive.trivia:
- 浄土真宗本願寺派の寺院で、JR・北陸新幹線金沢駅から徒歩約10分の市街地立地。
- 地獄極楽絵図は江戸時代より伝わる一具で、本堂で春秋の彼岸期に特別公開される。
- 絵図には八種の地獄（八大地獄）・六道輪廻・十種の極楽浄土が描かれる。
- 春彼岸は春分前後、秋彼岸は秋分前後の彼岸期に開催される短期特別公開行事。
- 専用駐車場はなく公共交通利用が望ましい。
- 金沢の王道観光ルート（金沢城・兼六園・東茶屋街）から外れた位置にあり、地獄絵・六道思想公開を主題に訪問する参詣者が中心。
deepdive.warnings_extra: 地獄極楽絵図ご開帳は春・秋の彼岸期間中の短期特別公開で、毎年の正式日程・公開時間・予約要否は変動するため、訪問前に照円寺公式・石川県観光公式で必ず確認する。本絵図は宗教的崇敬の対象で、近世の貴重な仏画であり、揶揄的・興味本位の観覧・SNS拡散は厳に慎み、宗教・参詣者・住職への敬意を最優先する。本堂内・絵図前は撮影禁止または制限される場合が多く、撮影可否・拝観作法は当日の寺院案内に厳格に従う。本寺には専用駐車場がないため、公共交通（JR・北陸新幹線金沢駅徒歩約10分）の利用が望ましく、自家用車利用時は近隣のコインパーキング利用となる。金沢市街地は冬季（12〜3月）に積雪が多く、特別公開が春彼岸に重なる場合は残雪・路面凍結への備えが必要。境内・本堂は段差・狭隘部を含み、参拝路は石畳・砂利道を含む場合があるため、歩きやすい靴での参詣が望ましい。最新の公開日程・予約条件は照円寺公式で必ず確認する。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Shouenji Temple is a Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-branch temple located at 1-20 Kasaichi-cho, Kanazawa City, Ishikawa Prefecture, approximately 10 minutes on foot from JR and Hokuriku Shinkansen Kanazawa Station, in an urban setting. According to the Shouenji official source and the Ishikawa Prefecture Tourism official source, the temple has been in possession of a suite of Hell and Paradise paintings (jigoku-gokuraku ezu) transmitted since the Edo period, which are specially displayed in the main hall to pilgrims during the special display period of the "Jigoku-gokuraku Ezu Go-kaichō" (Grand Hell and Paradise Painting Display) held during the spring and autumn equinox (Higan) period each year. The paintings depict the eight types of hell (Hacchi daijigoku: Toto, Kokujo, Shugo, Kyokan, Dakyokan, Shonetsu, Daishonetsu, Muken), the rokudō (six realms: hell, hungry ghosts, animals, fighting spirits, humans, heaven) cycle, and the ten types of the Pure Land — allowing visitors to see in one place how people of the pre-modern era visualized hell, paradise, karmic retribution, and rebirth in the Pure Land. The paintings are precious early modern relics representing the visualization of the Buddhist view of the afterlife, and the tradition of displaying them as the annual observance "Jigoku-gokuraku Ezu Go-kaichō" during the Higan season has been transmitted at Shouenji. While located in an urban Kanazawa temple, accessible from Kanazawa Station, it stands away from the mainstream tourist route (Kanazawa Castle, Kenrokuen, Higashi Chayamachi, Omicho Market), making it primarily visited by local residents, religious stakeholders, and pilgrims with an interest in hell paintings and rokudō thought during the special display period. [Shouenji Official](https://syouenji.jp), [Ishikawa Prefecture Tourism Official: Hot Ishikawa Travel Net](https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/spot/detail_22503.html)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Hell paintings and paradise paintings were produced and displayed at temples throughout Japan from the Heian to Edo periods, forming the core of an "etoki" (picture-explanation) culture in which Buddhist views of the afterlife — rokudō thought, Ten Kings veneration, and Pure Land Buddhism — were visualized to convey teachings of karmic retribution, good and evil deeds, and rebirth in the Pure Land even to those with low literacy. Remnants of hell and paradise paintings have been passed down nationally — Yamagoe Amida-zu at Konkaikomyo-ji (Kyoto), hell paintings at Zenkoji (Nagano), hell paintings at Kogawa-dera (Wakayama), Ten Kings scrolls at Rokuharamitsuji (Kyoto), the Dai Jigoku-e at Chogakuji (Nara, nine-scroll single suite) — but Shouenji's jigoku-gokuraku ezu is distinctive in its composition depicting eight types of hell, six realms, and ten types of the Pure Land in a single suite, and in its format of a short-term special display timed to the Buddhist annual observance of the Higan season. Higan (spring equinox: around the vernal equinox; autumn equinox: around the autumnal equinox) is an important Buddhist annual observance linked to rebirth in the Pure Land and ancestor veneration, and the act of displaying the jigoku-gokuraku ezu during the Higan season has served the religious function of having pilgrims directly experience views of the afterlife, karmic retribution, and rebirth in the Pure Land. The display of the jigoku-gokuraku ezu at a Jodo Shinshu temple is carried out in the context of Amida veneration and tariki-hongan (reliance on the power of another), playing a role of conveying the significance of Amida Nyorai's redemptive power and rebirth in the Pure Land through the contrast of hell and paradise.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Kasaichi-cho, Kanazawa City, is located on the west side of JR and Hokuriku Shinkansen Kanazawa Station in an urban area, forming one section of the district where early modern Jodo Shinshu and Hongwanji-branch temples are concentrated. Shouenji has been affectionately frequented by local residents, parishioners, and pilgrims from afar as a neighborhood urban temple near Kanazawa Station, and the Jigoku-gokuraku Ezu Go-kaichō during the spring and autumn Higan seasons is transmitted as an annual observance for local residents, parishioners, and pilgrims. As the temple stands away from Kanazawa's mainstream sightseeing (Kanazawa Castle, Kenrokuen, Higashi Chayamachi, Omicho Market, Kanazawa 21st Century Museum), visits are centered on those visiting during the special display period — local residents, religious stakeholders, and pilgrims with an interest in hell paintings and rokudō thought. Viewers are strongly expected to approach hell paintings with religious respect and a posture of quiet participation in the Higan religious annual observance. [Shouenji Official](https://syouenji.jp), [Ishikawa Prefecture Tourism Official: Hot Ishikawa Travel Net](https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/spot/detail_22503.html)

deepdive.best_visit_time_en: The Jigoku-gokuraku Ezu Go-kaichō is held during the special display period of the spring equinox (around the vernal equinox) and autumn equinox (around the autumnal equinox) each year. As the exact dates, opening hours, and reservation requirements for 2026 may change, always confirm with the Shouenji official source and the Ishikawa Prefecture Tourism official source before visiting. During the special display period, there may be crowds, and visiting in the relatively quiet weekday morning time slot is recommended. The spring equinox is around mid to late March and remaining snow may be found in the Kanazawa city center; rain gear and waterproof shoes are advisable. The autumn equinox is around mid to late September and the weather is relatively comfortable.
deepdive.photo_tips_en: The precinct and exterior of the main hall can generally be photographed, but photography inside the main hall and in front of the jigoku-gokuraku ezu is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography and flash permissions. Strictly refrain from photographing or sharing the paintings in a mocking, horror-purpose, or prurient manner, and prioritize respect for the early modern rokudō thought, karmic retribution, and rebirth in the Pure Land religious culture. Be mindful of the privacy of other pilgrims when photographing the jigoku-gokuraku ezu display; do not publish compositions with people in frame without consent. The temple is an urban temple; photograph from positions that do not obstruct the movements of local residents and pedestrians around the temple.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- A Jodo Shinshu Hongwanji-branch temple approximately 10 minutes on foot from JR and Hokuriku Shinkansen Kanazawa Station.
- The jigoku-gokuraku ezu is an Edo-period suite transmitted in the temple, specially displayed in the main hall during the spring and autumn Higan seasons.
- The paintings depict eight types of hell (Eight Great Hells), the rokudō (six realms) cycle, and ten types of the Pure Land.
- A short-term special display event held during the Higan season (spring equinox and autumn equinox).
- No dedicated parking; public transport use is recommended.
- Standing away from the mainstream Kanazawa sightseeing route (Kanazawa Castle, Kenrokuen, Higashi Chayamachi), visits are centered on those interested in hell paintings and rokudō thought.
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: The Jigoku-gokuraku Ezu Go-kaichō is a short-term special display during the spring and autumn Higan seasons, and the exact dates, opening hours, and reservation requirements change each year, so always confirm with the Shouenji official source and the Ishikawa Prefecture Tourism official source before visiting. The paintings are objects of religious reverence, precious early modern Buddhist paintings; mocking, prurient, or curiosity-seeking viewing and social media sharing are strictly to be avoided, and respect for religion, pilgrims, and the head priest must take precedence. Photography inside the main hall and in front of the paintings is often prohibited or restricted; strictly follow the temple's on-site guidance on photography permissions and worship protocol. As there is no dedicated parking at this temple, public transport (approximately 10 minutes on foot from JR and Hokuriku Shinkansen Kanazawa Station) is recommended; those using private vehicles should use nearby coin parking lots. Kanazawa City receives heavy snowfall in winter (December–March), and if the special display falls in the spring equinox period, preparations for remaining snow and road icing may be needed. The precinct and main hall may contain steps and narrow passages, and sections with stone paving and gravel; visiting in easy-to-walk shoes is recommended. Always confirm the latest display dates, reservation conditions, etc. with the Shouenji official source.

name: お松権現社（猫神さん）
name_kana: おまつごんげんしゃ（ねこがみさん）
address: 〒779-1240 徳島県阿南市加茂町不け63
fee: 境内自由（公式サイト掲載の祈祷料・授与品料金に準ずる）
hours: 境内通年自由参拝（社務所対応時間は公式サイト掲載に準ずる）
summary: 徳島県阿南市加茂町に鎮座する、別名『猫神さん』として信仰される神社。お松権現社公式の由緒によれば、江戸前期の阿波・加茂村で、不当な代官の取り立てに苦しんだ村の女性『お松』が直訴の末に処刑され、その無念を晴らすために飼い猫が怪猫となって代官の家を災いに陥れたとの伝承が伝わる。後に村人がお松を権現として祀り、飼い猫の霊をも合祀したのが当社の縁起とされ、近世以来『猫神さん』として、訴訟・勝負事・願掛けの神として信仰されてきた。社殿の周囲には参拝者が奉納した約一万体ともいわれる招き猫が並び、参道には狛犬ならぬ『狛猫』が鎮座する。怪猫伝承・猫神信仰・大量の招き猫奉納というB級スポット的要素を持つ徳島県の代表的な民俗信仰の場の一つ。
highlights: 江戸前期のお松直訴伝承と飼い猫の怪猫伝承を背景とする『猫神さん』
社殿周囲に奉納された約一万体ともいわれる招き猫群
狛犬ならぬ『狛猫』が守る猫神信仰の社
from_kameyama: 車で約4時間（東名阪→新名神→神戸淡路鳴門自動車道→徳島道→徳島南部・那賀方面）／新幹線＋特急で約5時間（亀山→新大阪→新神戸→高速バスまたは特急『うずしお』徳島駅→JR牟岐線阿南駅→タクシー）
category_label: 猫神・怪猫伝承・招き猫奉納・B級民俗信仰
safety_note: お松権現社は徳島県阿南市加茂町の集落内に鎮座する社で、境内・参道は段差・砂利・狭隘部を含むため歩きやすい靴での参拝が望ましい。社殿周囲・境内には参拝者奉納の大量の招き猫が並び、像の落下・破損を避けるため触れる・動かす行為は慎む。本社は怪猫伝承・お松直訴伝承を背景とする宗教施設で、伝承は『縁起では』『伝承では』と扱われ、史実として断定する表現は避ける。参拝時は他参拝者・氏子・神職への敬意ある所作を心がけ、揶揄的・興味本位な撮影・SNS拡散は厳に慎む。集落内立地で駐車場・道路は狭隘なため、自家用車利用時は係員・公式案内の指示に従い、住宅地内の路上駐車は避ける。山間集落のため、降雨時は路面状況に注意し、無理な訪問は避ける。

deepdive.history_jp: お松権現社は、徳島県阿南市加茂町不け63に鎮座する『猫神さん』として信仰される神社で、お松権現社公式の由緒によれば、江戸前期の阿波・加茂村に伝わるお松の直訴伝承と飼い猫の怪猫伝承を縁起とする。伝承では、江戸前期の加茂村で、不当な代官の取り立てに苦しんだ村の女性『お松』が、村を救うため代官の不正を直訴したが、逆に罪に問われ処刑されたという。お松の無念を晴らすために、生前可愛がっていた飼い猫が怪猫となって代官の家に取り憑き、代官の家を災いに陥れたと伝わり、後に村人がお松の霊を権現として祀り、飼い猫の霊をも合祀したのが当社の縁起とされる。近世以来『猫神さん』として、訴訟・勝負事・願掛け・諸願成就の神として信仰されてきた。社殿の周囲には参拝者が奉納した約一万体ともいわれる招き猫が並び、参道には狛犬ならぬ『狛猫』が鎮座しており、猫を主役とする神社として全国的にも稀有な存在として知られる。本伝承は史実として確認された事柄ではなく『縁起では』『伝承では』として位置付けられている。[お松権現社公式 由緒](https://www.nekogami.jp/history.php)、[お松権現社公式](https://www.nekogami.jp)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 猫を祭神・神格とする神社・祠は、東京・今戸神社（招き猫発祥伝承）、東京・自性院（招き猫地蔵）、新潟・南部神社、京都・梨木神社の猫塚、佐賀・秀林禅寺の鍋島の化け猫塚など全国に分布するが、お松権現社のように、人間（お松）と飼い猫の双方を合祀し、怪猫伝承を縁起として明文化し、参拝者奉納の招き猫が一万体規模で蓄積し、狛犬の代わりに狛猫が鎮座する形態は全国でも稀有である。怪猫伝承は江戸期の歌舞伎・読本・浮世絵などに数多く題材化された民俗的モチーフで、佐賀の鍋島の化け猫、岡山の有馬の化け猫、福岡・有馬家の怪猫など、地方ごとに固有の怪猫伝承が形成された。お松権現社の伝承は、不当な権力（代官）に苦しむ庶民の無念を、人間の女性とその飼い猫の連携で晴らすという民衆的・抵抗的物語構造を持ち、近世民衆の権力・正義観を反映する民俗的物語として民俗学・宗教社会学の関心を引いてきた。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 徳島県阿南市加茂町は那賀川・桑野川の沿岸に広がる地区で、お松権現社は加茂町不け地区の集落内に鎮座する。地元住民にとって、当社は猫神さん・お松様として親しまれ、訴訟・勝負事・願掛け・諸願成就の神として近世以来継承されてきた。社殿周囲の招き猫奉納は近年特に増加し、四国・関西を中心とした参拝者・愛猫家・信者からの奉納で構成される。観光客の自由な参拝も受け入れているが、集落内立地・駐車場の限り・住宅地が隣接する立地のため、参拝者は係員・公式案内の指示に従い、地区住民の生活空間への配慮ある参拝姿勢が強く期待される。[お松権現社公式](https://www.nekogami.jp)、[お松権現社 由緒](https://www.nekogami.jp/history.php)

- 猫信仰・招き猫文化に関する民俗学研究文献

deepdive.best_visit_time: 通年参拝可。春・秋の比較的気候が穏やかな時期が屋外散策に向く。夏季は高温多湿で熱中症対策が必要、冬季は山あいの集落で冷え込むため防寒着が必要。お盆・正月・祭礼日は混雑する場合があるため、平日午前中の比較的静謐な時間帯の参拝が望ましい。
deepdive.photo_tips: 社殿・招き猫奉納群・狛猫は当社の象徴的被写体だが、奉納された招き猫像は他参拝者の信仰の表現であり、像を移動させる・触れる・破損させる行為は厳に慎む。撮影時は他参拝者のプライバシーに配慮し、人物が映り込む構図は同意なく公開しない。境内・社殿内・参道での撮影マナー（フラッシュ可否・三脚利用可否）は公式案内・現地表示に従う。揶揄的・興味本位な構図・SNS拡散は厳に慎み、お松伝承・猫神信仰・参拝者・地区住民への敬意を最優先する。
deepdive.trivia:
- 別名『猫神さん』として親しまれる徳島県阿南市の神社。
- 縁起は江戸前期のお松直訴伝承と飼い猫の怪猫伝承に基づく。
- 社殿周囲には参拝者奉納の招き猫が約一万体並ぶとされる。
- 参道には狛犬ではなく『狛猫』が鎮座する。
- 訴訟・勝負事・願掛け・諸願成就の神として近世以来信仰されてきた。
- 公式サイトで由緒が公開されている。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Omatsu Gongen-sha (Omatsu Gongen Shrine) is a shrine in Fuke 63, Kamo-cho, Anan City, Tokushima Prefecture, venerated as the "Neko-gami-san" (Cat Deity), whose engi (foundation legend) is based on the tradition of Omatsu's direct remonstrance (jikiso) to the authorities and the legend of a spirit cat (bakemono neko) transmitted in Kamo Village of Awa Province in the early Edo period. According to the official provenance of Omatsu Gongen-sha, in early Edo Kamo Village, a woman named "Omatsu" suffered under an unjust official's exactions, and, to save the village, filed a direct complaint (jikiso) against the official's corruption — but was instead charged with a crime and executed. According to tradition, the cat Omatsu had kept as a pet, driven by resentment over Omatsu's unjust treatment, became a spirit cat that haunted the official's household and brought disaster upon it; and later the villagers enshrined Omatsu's spirit as a gongen (a manifestation of a Buddha in the guise of a kami), and also co-enshrined the cat's spirit — this being the origin of the shrine. Since the early modern period, it has been venerated as the "Neko-gami-san" for winning lawsuits, competition, vows, and the fulfillment of various wishes. Surrounding the shrine building are approximately 10,000 beckoning-cat (maneki-neko) statues said to have been donated by pilgrims; the approach path is guarded by "koma-neko" (guardian cats) in the place of the usual lion-dog (komainu) statues, making it nationally known as a rare example of a shrine where cats are the protagonists. This tradition is not presented as established historical fact but as folklore ("according to tradition," "according to the engi"). [Omatsu Gongen-sha Official: Provenance](https://www.nekogami.jp/history.php), [Omatsu Gongen-sha Official](https://www.nekogami.jp)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Shrines and small halls in which cats are the deity or sacred being are distributed throughout Japan — Imado Jinja (Tokyo, beckoning cat origin tradition), Jisho-in (Tokyo, beckoning cat Jizo), Nanbu Jinja (Niigata), the cat mound at Nashinoki Jinja (Kyoto), and the Nabeshima bakeneko burial mound at Shurin Zenji (Saga), among others — but a form like Omatsu Gongen-sha, in which both a human (Omatsu) and her cat are co-enshrined, with the spirit cat tradition explicitly written out as the engi, with approximately 10,000 donated maneki-neko accumulated, and koma-neko guarding the approach path in place of komainu, is nationally rare. The spirit cat tradition was extensively used as subject matter in Edo-period kabuki, kusazoshi, and ukiyo-e, and Nabeshima's bakeneko (Saga), Arima's bakeneko (Okayama), and Arima-ke's spirit cat (Fukuoka) are representative examples, with each region forming its own distinctive spirit cat tradition. The tradition of Omatsu Gongen-sha has a folk narrative structure of popular resistance and justice — in which the resentment of a village woman (Omatsu) and her cat at injustice by authority (the official) is collectively vindicated — and has attracted interest in folklore and religious sociology as a folkloric narrative reflecting the early modern commoner view of authority and justice.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Anan City, Tokushima Prefecture, Kamo-cho, is a district spreading along the banks of the Naka River and Kuwa-no River, and Omatsu Gongen-sha is enshrined within the settlement of the Fuke district of Kamo-cho. For local residents, the shrine has been affectionately known as the "Neko-gami-san" and "Omatsu-sama" and has been transmitted since the early modern period as a deity for winning lawsuits, competition, and the fulfillment of various wishes. Donated maneki-neko have increased particularly in recent years, consisting of donations from pilgrims and cat lovers centered on Shikoku and the Kansai region. While the shrine accepts free visits from tourists, as it is located within a settlement, with limited parking and adjacent residential areas, pilgrims are expected to follow the instructions of attendants and official guidance and approach the residential space of the district with a posture of respectful consideration. [Omatsu Gongen-sha Official](https://www.nekogami.jp), [Omatsu Gongen-sha Official: Provenance](https://www.nekogami.jp/history.php)

deepdive.best_visit_time_en: Worship is possible year-round. Spring and autumn, when the weather is relatively mild, are suitable for outdoor strolling. Summer is hot and humid requiring heat stroke countermeasures; winter in the mountain valley settlement is cold requiring warm clothing. During Obon, New Year, and festival days, crowds may occur; weekday mornings are relatively quiet for worship.
deepdive.photo_tips_en: The shrine building, donated maneki-neko group, and koma-neko are symbolic subjects of this shrine, but donated maneki-neko statues are expressions of other pilgrims' faith; handling, touching, moving, or damaging statues is strictly to be avoided. Be mindful of the privacy of other pilgrims and do not publish compositions with people in frame without consent. Photography etiquette inside the precinct and shrine building, and along the approach path (flash permissions, tripod use permissions), should follow the official guide and on-site signage. Strictly refrain from mocking, prurient, or ironic compositions and social media sharing, and prioritize respect for the Omatsu tradition, cat deity veneration, pilgrims, and district residents.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- A Tokushima Prefecture, Anan City, shrine affectionately known as the "Neko-gami-san" (Cat Deity).
- The engi is based on the early Edo period Omatsu direct remonstrance tradition and the spirit cat tradition of a pet cat.
- Approximately 10,000 donated maneki-neko statues by pilgrims are said to line the precinct.
- "Koma-neko" (guardian cats) guard the approach path in place of the usual komainu (lion-dog statues).
- Venerated as a deity for winning lawsuits, competition, vows, and the fulfillment of various wishes since the early modern period.
- The provenance is publicly available on the official website.
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: This shrine is a religious facility based on the spirit cat tradition and the Omatsu direct remonstrance tradition; the tradition is positioned as "according to tradition" and "according to the engi," and expressions asserting it as historical fact should be avoided. At worship, maintain a posture of respect toward other pilgrims, ujiko (parishioners), and shrine priests; strictly refrain from mocking, prurient, or entertainment-seeking photography and social media sharing. The large number of donated maneki-neko statues filling the precinct and shrine building area should not be touched, moved, or damaged, as they are expressions of other pilgrims' faith. The shrine is in a settlement with limited parking and narrow roads; those visiting by private vehicle should follow the instructions of attendants and official guidance and avoid roadside parking within the residential area. Mountain valley settlement; note road conditions during rain, and avoid unreasonable visits. The precinct and approach path contain steps, gravel, and narrow sections; visiting in easy-to-walk shoes is recommended. Always confirm the latest worship conditions and shrine office response hours on the official website.

name: 多賀神社凸凹神堂
name_kana: たがじんじゃでこぼこしんどう
address: 〒798-0040 愛媛県宇和島市藤江1340
status: 現存・有料拝観（神堂・性文化財資料館は未成年入館不可）
fee: 公式サイト掲載の拝観料に準ずる（神社境内参拝は自由、凸凹神堂・性文化財資料館は別途有料・要事前確認）
hours: 公式サイト・愛媛県観光案内掲載の開館時間に準ずる
summary: 愛媛県宇和島市藤江に鎮座する多賀神社の境内に併設される性信仰の文化資料館で、別名『凸凹神堂』として知られる。宇和島市観光協会・愛媛県観光物産協会の案内によれば、本社は古来より延命栄寿・治病・子授け・健康の霊験で知られる多賀大神を祀り、参拝者の奉納・寄贈による性に関する民俗資料・美術工芸品を収蔵・展示する『性文化財資料館』を併設している。多賀大神への子授け・夫婦和合・無病息災の信仰を背景に、近世以来の春画・性的造形物・性に関わる民具・郷土玩具などが体系的に収蔵されており、性を子授け・繁栄・健康の象徴として捉える日本の民俗信仰の文脈で位置付けられる施設として知られる。未成年（18歳未満）は神堂・資料館への入館不可。
highlights: 多賀大神を祀る延命栄寿・治病・子授け・健康の社
境内に併設される性文化財資料館『凸凹神堂』
性を子授け・繁栄・健康の象徴として捉える日本の民俗信仰の収蔵展示
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間（東名阪→新名神→神戸淡路鳴門自動車道→松山道→宇和島道）／新幹線＋特急で約7時間（亀山→新大阪→新神戸→特急『しおかぜ』松山駅→特急『宇和海』宇和島駅→タクシー）
category_label: 性信仰・子授け・延命栄寿・性文化財資料館・民俗信仰
safety_note: 多賀神社凸凹神堂は愛媛県宇和島市藤江の市街地に鎮座する神社で、境内参拝は通年自由だが、神堂・性文化財資料館は未成年（18歳未満）の入館不可・有料の専門展示施設である。展示物には性に関わる近世以来の民俗資料・美術工芸品が含まれ、宗教的・民俗的文脈での収蔵展示として位置付けられる。観覧時は揶揄的・興味本位な観覧・SNS拡散を厳に慎み、子授け・繁栄・健康を願う民俗信仰の文脈への敬意ある観覧姿勢を保つ。展示物の撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の館内案内に厳格に従い、許可されない場合は撮影しない。境内・参道は段差・狭隘部を含むため歩きやすい靴での参拝が望ましい。アクセスはJR予讃線宇和島駅からタクシー、自家用車の場合は松山自動車道宇和島朝日ICから約10分。

deepdive.history_jp: 多賀神社凸凹神堂は、愛媛県宇和島市藤江1340に鎮座する多賀神社の境内に併設される性信仰の文化資料館で、宇和島市観光協会・愛媛県観光物産協会の案内によれば、本社は古来より延命栄寿・治病・子授け・健康の霊験で知られる多賀大神（伊弉諾尊・伊弉冉尊）を祀り、参拝者の奉納・寄贈による性に関する民俗資料・美術工芸品を収蔵・展示する『性文化財資料館』を併設している。多賀大神への子授け・夫婦和合・無病息災の信仰を背景に、近世以来の春画・性的造形物・性に関わる民具・郷土玩具などが体系的に収蔵されており、性を子授け・繁栄・健康の象徴として捉える日本の民俗信仰の文脈で位置付けられる施設として知られる。神堂・性文化財資料館は未成年（18歳未満）の入館不可で、宗教的・民俗的文脈での収蔵展示として位置付けられる専門施設である。[宇和島市観光協会](https://www.uwajima.org/spot/index6.html)、[いよ観ネット 多賀神社凸凹神堂](https://www.iyokannet.jp/spot/1813)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 性を子授け・繁栄・健康・厄除けの象徴として神格化する民俗信仰は、日本各地に分布し、新潟・栃尾のほだれ祭、岩手・金勢様、愛知・田縣神社の豊年祭、愛知・大縣神社の豊年祭、神奈川・かなまら祭など多様な形態を持つ。多賀神社凸凹神堂は、この性信仰を、祭礼ではなく『性文化財資料館』として近世以来の資料・造形物・民具を体系的に収蔵展示する施設として独自に発展させた点で全国でも稀有な事例で、性信仰・性民俗・性風俗史の研究・教育に資する民俗資料の集積として民俗学・宗教社会学・性風俗史研究の対象となっている。多賀大神は近江国・多賀大社（滋賀県多賀町）を本社とする延命栄寿の神で、伊弉諾尊・伊弉冉尊を祀ることから子授け・夫婦和合の信仰と結び付き、全国各地に分社・分祠を持つ。宇和島の多賀神社はその四国における主要な分祠の一つで、性文化財資料館の併設は、子授け・繁栄を司る多賀大神の神格と性民俗の体系的収蔵を結び付ける民俗信仰の現代的継承の事例として位置付けられる。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 愛媛県宇和島市藤江は宇和海に面した宇和島市の市街地区で、多賀神社凸凹神堂は地元では『お多賀さん』として、延命・子授け・健康の神として近世以来親しまれてきた神社である。性文化財資料館の併設は近代以降の取組で、地元の宗教関係者・住民・郷土史家による収蔵・展示の継続努力により、現在の体系的な民俗資料館としての姿が形成されてきた。観光客の訪問も受け入れるが、本施設は宗教的・民俗的文脈での性文化財資料館として位置付けられ、観覧者には民俗信仰への敬意・揶揄的観覧の自制・近世以来の性民俗を巡る歴史的文脈への理解ある観覧姿勢が強く期待される。[宇和島市観光協会](https://www.uwajima.org/spot/index6.html)、[いよ観ネット 多賀神社凸凹神堂](https://www.iyokannet.jp/spot/1813)
deepdive.related_works:
- 宇和島市観光協会公式ページ
- いよ観ネット（愛媛県観光物産協会）多賀神社凸凹神堂ページ
- 多賀大社（滋賀県多賀町）関連資料
- 性信仰・性民俗・性風俗史に関する民俗学・宗教社会学研究文献
- 日本各地の性信仰祭礼・施設（ほだれ祭・田縣神社豊年祭・大縣神社豊年祭・かなまら祭等）の比較研究文献

[いよ観ネット 多賀神社凸凹神堂](https://www.iyokannet.jp/spot/1813)
deepdive.best_visit_time: 通年参拝可（神堂・性文化財資料館は開館時間内のみ、開館日要事前確認）。春・秋の気候が穏やかな時期が散策に向く。夏季は高温多湿のため熱中症対策が必要、冬季は冷え込みに対する防寒準備が必要。
deepdive.photo_tips: 境内・社殿外観は撮影可能だが、神堂・性文化財資料館内の展示物の撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の館内案内に厳格に従い、許可されない場合は撮影しない。展示物は宗教的・民俗的文脈での収蔵展示であり、揶揄的・興味本位・性的興味本位な構図・SNS拡散は厳に慎む。子授け・繁栄・健康を願う民俗信仰の文脈への敬意ある撮影姿勢を保ち、他参拝者・観覧者のプライバシーに配慮する。
deepdive.trivia:
- 多賀神社は近江・多賀大社の四国における分祠の一つで、多賀大神（伊弉諾尊・伊弉冉尊）を祀る。
- 別名『凸凹神堂』として知られる性文化財資料館を併設する。
- 神堂・性文化財資料館は未成年（18歳未満）入館不可。
- 近世以来の春画・性的造形物・性に関わる民具・郷土玩具などを体系的に収蔵展示。
- 延命栄寿・治病・子授け・健康の霊験で古来から信仰される。
- 民俗信仰の文脈での性文化財収蔵展示としては全国でも稀有な施設。
deepdive.warnings_extra: 本社の境内参拝は通年自由だが、神堂・性文化財資料館は未成年（18歳未満）の入館不可・有料の専門展示施設である。展示物には性に関わる近世以来の民俗資料・美術工芸品が含まれ、宗教的・民俗的文脈での収蔵展示として位置付けられる。観覧時は揶揄的・興味本位・性的興味本位な観覧・SNS拡散を厳に慎み、子授け・繁栄・健康を願う民俗信仰の文脈への敬意ある観覧姿勢を保つ。展示物の撮影可否・フラッシュ可否は当日の館内案内に厳格に従い、許可されない場合は撮影しない。境内・参道は段差・狭隘部を含むため歩きやすい靴での参拝が望ましい。アクセスはJR予讃線宇和島駅からタクシー、自家用車の場合は松山自動車道宇和島朝日ICから約10分で、駐車場利用条件は公式案内で事前に確認する。開館日・開館時間は不定期に変動する場合があるため、訪問前に公式・愛媛県観光案内で必ず確認する。最新の入館条件・拝観料・開館日は公式サイトで必ず確認する。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Taga Jinja Dekoboko Shindo is a cultural archive of sexuality beliefs attached to the precinct of Taga Jinja (Taga Shrine) at 1340 Fujie, Uwajima City, Ehime Prefecture. According to the Uwajima City Tourism Association and the Ehime Prefecture Tourism and Products Association guides, the main shrine has been known since ancient times for its miraculous powers in longevity, cure of illness, granting of children, and health, and enshrines Taga Okami (Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto); it is co-located with a "Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan" (Cultural Archive of Sexuality) housing and displaying folk-cultural materials and art and craft objects related to sexuality donated and bequeathed by pilgrims. Against the background of faith in Taga Okami for granting children, marital harmony, and freedom from illness, the archive systematically houses early modern shunga (erotic prints), sexually-themed sculptural objects, folk implements related to sexuality, and regional folk toys — positioned as a facility understood within the context of the Japanese folk belief framework of viewing sexuality as a symbol of granting children, prosperity, and health. The Shindo (archive hall) and the Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan do not permit entry to minors (under 18 years of age) and are positioned as specialized facilities for housing and displaying items in a religious and folkloric context. [Uwajima City Tourism Association](https://www.uwajima.org/spot/index6.html), [Iyo-kan Net: Taga Jinja Dekoboko Shindo](https://www.iyokannet.jp/spot/1813)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: Folk belief that deifies sexuality as a symbol of granting children, prosperity, health, and warding off evil is distributed throughout Japan, with diverse forms including Hodare Matsuri in Tochio, Niigata; Kinsei-sama in Iwate; Honen-sai at Tagata Jinja (Aichi); Honen-sai at Oagata Jinja (Aichi); Kanamara Matsuri (Kanagawa), and many others. Taga Jinja Dekoboko Shindo is a nationally rare case in which this sexuality belief is developed as a specialized facility that systematically houses and displays materials, sculptural objects, and folk implements related to sexuality from the early modern era — rather than as a festival — making it an object of folklore, religious sociology, and the history of sexual customs research and education as an accumulation of folk materials related to sexuality belief, folk customs, and the history of sexual customs. Taga Okami is the deity of the Taga Taisha (Taga Grand Shrine) in Omi Province (Taga-cho, Shiga Prefecture), the principal shrine for longevity, and enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto; connected with faith in granting children and marital harmony, it has branch shrines and smaller shrines throughout Japan. Uwajima's Taga Jinja is one of the major branch shrines in Shikoku, and the attachment of the Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan represents a case study of the modern transmission of folk belief in which the systematic housing of the archive is linked to the divine character of Taga Okami as the deity of granting children and prosperity.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Fujie, Uwajima City, Ehime Prefecture, is an urban district facing the Uwa Sea of Uwajima City, and Taga Jinja Dekoboko Shindo is locally affectionately known as "the Otaga-san," venerated as a deity of longevity, granting children, and health since the early modern period. The attachment of the Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan is a modern effort, and the current form as a systematic folk materials archive has been shaped by the ongoing efforts of religious stakeholders, local residents, and regional historians to house and display the materials. The facility accepts visits from tourists, but as a religious and folkloric context for the Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan, viewers are strongly expected to approach the folk belief on sexuality with reverence, to refrain from mocking viewing, and to approach the display with a historical contextual understanding of early modern sexuality folk customs. [Uwajima City Tourism Association](https://www.uwajima.org/spot/index6.html), [Iyo-kan Net: Taga Jinja Dekoboko Shindo](https://www.iyokannet.jp/spot/1813)

deepdive.best_visit_time_en: Year-round worship is possible (the Shindo and Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan are open only during opening hours; confirm opening days in advance). Spring and autumn, when the weather is mild, are suitable for outdoor strolling. Summer is hot and humid requiring heat stroke countermeasures; winter requires warm clothing.
deepdive.photo_tips_en: The precinct and exterior of the shrine building can generally be photographed, but photography permissions and flash permissions for displayed objects inside the Shindo and Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan must strictly follow the in-hall guidance on the day; do not photograph if not permitted. Displayed objects are religious and folkloric context archival displays; strictly refrain from mocking, prurient, or sexuality-interest-only compositions and social media sharing. Maintain a posture of respectful photography in the context of the folk belief of seeking the blessings of granting children, prosperity, and health, and be mindful of the privacy of other pilgrims and viewers.
deepdive.trivia_en:
- Taga Jinja is one of the major Shikoku branch shrines of the Taga Taisha in Omi Province, enshrining Taga Okami (Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto).
- Known by the nickname "Dekoboko Shindo" (Convex-Concave Sacred Hall) and co-located with a Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan (Cultural Archive of Sexuality).
- The Shindo and Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan do not permit entry to minors (under 18 years of age).
- Systematically houses and displays early modern shunga, sexually-themed sculptural objects, folk implements related to sexuality, and regional folk toys.
- Venerated since ancient times for longevity, cure of illness, granting of children, and health.
- A nationally rare facility for housing and displaying sexuality folk-cultural materials in a religious and folkloric context.
deepdive.warnings_extra_en: While year-round free worship of the main shrine precinct is possible, the Shindo and Sei Bunkazai Shiryokan do not permit entry to minors (under 18 years of age) and are paid specialized exhibition facilities. Displayed objects include folk materials, art, and craft objects related to sexuality from the early modern era, positioned as archival displays in a religious and folkloric context. At viewing, strictly refrain from mocking, prurient, or sexuality-interest-only viewing and social media sharing, and maintain a posture of respectful viewing in the context of the folk belief of seeking the blessings of granting children, prosperity, and health. Photography permissions and flash permissions for displayed objects must strictly follow the in-hall guidance on the day; do not photograph if not permitted. The precinct and approach path contain steps and narrow sections; visiting in easy-to-walk shoes is recommended. Access is approximately 10 minutes by taxi from JR Yosan Line Uwajima Station; by private vehicle, approximately 10 minutes from the Uwajima Asahi IC on the Matsuyama Expressway; parking conditions should be confirmed with the official guide in advance. Opening days and opening hours may change irregularly, so always confirm with the official source and Ehime Prefecture tourism guide before visiting. Always confirm the latest entry conditions, admission fees, and opening days on the official website.

name: 韓竈神社
name_kana: からかまじんじゃ
address: 〒691-0011 島根県出雲市唐川町408
status: 現存・通年参拝可（参道は険しい山道・岩の隙間）
hours: 公式案内に準ずる（日中の明るい時間帯のみの参拝を推奨）
summary: 島根県出雲市唐川町の山中に鎮座する古社。出雲市・島根県神社庁の案内によれば、出雲国風土記・延喜式神名帳にも見える由緒ある神社で、祭神は素盞嗚命（スサノオノミコト）。素盞嗚命が新羅から船で渡来した際に乗ってきたとされる岩船が境内裏にあるという伝承や、新羅由来の鉄・釜（竈）の文化と結び付くと伝わる古社。参道は約300段の石段と、本殿手前の幅45cmほどの巨大な岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する険しい構造で知られ、舗装されておらず携帯電話の電波もほぼ届かない山中の社として、岩の隙間参道・鉄文化伝承・素盞嗚命信仰を主軸に位置付けられる。
highlights: 出雲国風土記・延喜式神名帳に記される素盞嗚命を祀る古社
本殿手前の幅45cm程度の巨大な岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する険しい参道
新羅由来の鉄・竈（かま）の文化と結び付くと伝わる山中の古社
from_kameyama: 車で約6時間（東名阪→新名神→中国道→米子道・米子自動車道→山陰道→出雲ICまたは斐川IC経由・山道走行）／新幹線＋特急で約7時間（亀山→新大阪→新幹線『さくら』『みずほ』岡山→特急『やくも』出雲市駅→タクシー・レンタカーで唐川町方面）。最寄り駅から韓竈神社登り口まではタクシー・レンタカーが必須で、登り口から本殿までは徒歩約30〜60分（往復約1〜2時間）の山道。
category_label: 古社・素盞嗚命・新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化・岩の隙間参道・山中の社
safety_note: 韓竈神社は島根県出雲市唐川町の山中に鎮座する古社で、参拝には登り口から本殿まで徒歩約30〜60分の山道・約300段の石段・本殿手前の幅45cmほどの巨大な岩の隙間を抜ける険しい行程が必要である。参道は舗装されておらず、岩場・木の根・濡れた斜面を含み、通常のスニーカーでも歩行可能だが、滑りにくい登山靴・歩きやすい服装・両手が空くリュック・水分・行動食・ヘッドライト・救急用品・モバイルバッテリーが推奨される。本殿手前の岩の隙間は幅約45cmと非常に狭く、体格・荷物によっては通過困難な場合があるため、無理せず引き返す判断も必要。携帯電話の電波はほぼ届かないため、家族等への行程連絡・登山届相当の準備を行い、複数人で計画的に参拝する。悪天候時（雨・雪・凍結・霧）は参道が極めて危険となるため、参拝を避ける。日没後の参道は照明がなく非常に危険なため、必ず日中の明るい時間帯のみ参拝する。アクセスはJR山陰本線出雲市駅からタクシー・レンタカー、自家用車の場合は山陰道出雲ICまたは斐川ICから約30〜40分で、登り口の駐車場の利用条件は事前に確認する。本社は出雲市・島根県神社庁の公式案内で紹介される古社であり、揶揄的・興味本位な参拝・撮影・SNS拡散は慎み、出雲国風土記・延喜式神名帳に記される由緒・素盞嗚命信仰・新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化への敬意ある参拝姿勢を最優先する。

deepdive.history_jp: 韓竈神社は、島根県出雲市唐川町408の山中に鎮座する古社で、出雲市・島根県神社庁の案内によれば、出雲国風土記（733年）の出雲郡の項に『韓銍社』として、延喜式神名帳（927年）にも『韓竈神社』として記される由緒ある古社で、祭神は素盞嗚命（スサノオノミコト）である。素盞嗚命が新羅から船で渡来した際に乗ってきたとされる岩船が境内裏にあるという伝承や、新羅由来の鉄・釜（竈）の文化と結び付くと伝わり、社名の『韓竈』は『韓（から）の竈（かま）』を意味し、古代の朝鮮半島由来の製鉄・鉄文化との関連が示唆される。参道は登り口から本殿まで約300段の石段と、本殿手前の幅45cmほどの巨大な岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する険しい構造で知られ、舗装されておらず、携帯電話の電波もほぼ届かない山中の社として位置付けられる。本社は出雲市・島根県神社庁により公式に紹介される島根を代表する古社の一つで、出雲大社系の王道的な大社とは異なり、山中の険しい参道・素盞嗚命信仰・新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化と結び付く独特の参拝体験を提供する稀有な神社として知られる。[出雲市 韓竈神社](https://www.city.izumo.shimane.jp/www/contents/1610698269433/index.html)、[島根県神社庁 韓竈神社](https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/izumo/a8887760bc82186da6b7257897e32284bf08cbd7.html)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp: 出雲地方は古代以来、出雲国風土記・古事記・日本書紀に記される神話の地で、素盞嗚命・大国主命・天照大神に関わる神話・伝承が集積し、出雲大社・須佐神社・八重垣神社・神魂神社など多くの古社が現存する。韓竈神社は、その中でも素盞嗚命の新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化との結び付きが特に強く伝わる神社で、社名の『韓竈』が示すように朝鮮半島由来の製鉄・鉄文化・釜（竈）信仰との関連が古来指摘されてきた。古代出雲は中国地方の脊梁山地で良質な砂鉄を産し、たたら製鉄文化の中心地として近世まで栄えた地域で、製鉄・鉄器・釜（竈）は出雲文化の基盤を成してきた。韓竈神社の社名・祭神・伝承は、この出雲の鉄文化と朝鮮半島・新羅との文化的交流の記憶を伝える神社として、神話学・古代史・製鉄史・宗教社会学の研究対象となっている。また、本殿手前の岩の隙間を抜けて参拝する形式は、岩場・洞窟・隙間といった地形を神聖視する『磐座（いわくら）信仰』『岩戸信仰』とも結び付き、参拝者の身体を岩の隙間に通すことで日常から神域へ移行する民俗的・象徴的所作として位置付けられる稀有な参拝形式である。出雲大社系の大規模で整備された神社参拝とは対照的に、山中の険しい参道・岩の隙間・素盞嗚命信仰・新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化が重なる独自性の高い古社として、近年は神話・古代史・参拝文化に関心を持つ参拝者の間で注目を集めている。
deepdive.local_perspective_jp: 島根県出雲市唐川町は、出雲市の中央部やや北寄りの山間部に位置する集落で、近世以来たたら製鉄・林業・農業で栄えた地域である。韓竈神社は地元唐川町の住民・氏子・地区共同体により世代を超えて継承されてきた古社で、地区のアイデンティティを成す氏神として位置付けられている。参道の整備・本殿の維持・祭礼の進行は氏子・地区世話人・神職が役割を分担して代々継承してきた。観光客・参拝者の自由な訪問は受け入れるが、本社は山中の険しい参道・岩の隙間・素盞嗚命信仰・新羅渡来伝承・鉄文化と結び付く特殊な参拝体験を提供する神社であり、参拝者には山道・岩場での安全確保、参道・本殿への配慮、地区共同体・氏子・神職への敬意ある参拝姿勢が強く期待される。出雲大社系の王道参拝とは異なる文脈で位置付けられ、揶揄的・興味本位な参拝・撮影・SNS拡散は厳に慎み、神話・古代史・神社の由緒への理解、地区共同体の継承努力への尊重ある参拝姿勢が求められる。[出雲市 韓竈神社](https://www.city.izumo.shimane.jp/www/contents/1610698269433/index.html)、[島根県神社庁 韓竈神社](https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/izumo/a8887760bc82186da6b7257897e32284bf08cbd7.html)

deepdive.best_visit_time: 通年参拝可能だが、参道は舗装されていない山道・岩場・約300段の石段・本殿手前の岩の隙間を含むため、晴天の日中（特に春・秋の乾燥した穏やかな日）が参拝に適する。夏季は熱中症・夕立・落雷、冬季は積雪・凍結、梅雨・台風時は土砂崩れ・倒木の危険があるため避ける。日没後は参道が真っ暗で照明がなく非常に危険なため、必ず日中の明るい時間帯のみ参拝する。
deepdive.photo_tips: 本殿・岩の隙間参道・約300段の石段・山中の境内は本社の象徴的被写体だが、参道は舗装されておらず、岩場・木の根・濡れた斜面を含むため、撮影時の足元注意が必須。撮影機材を持ち上げての石段昇降・岩の隙間通過は転倒・落下・破損の危険があるため、軽量な機材を選び、両手が空くリュック・カメラストラップで確実に固定する。本殿・岩の隙間・参道の撮影は、他の参拝者の動線・神域への配慮、地区共同体・氏子・神職への敬意を最優先し、フラッシュ・ドローン撮影は厳禁。揶揄的・興味本位な撮影・SNS拡散は慎み、神話・古代史・神社の由緒への敬意ある撮影姿勢を保つ。携帯電話の電波がほぼ届かない山中の社であるため、撮影画像のクラウド自動アップロード等は機能しない可能性が高く、撮影後の管理に注意する。

deepdive.history_jp_en: Karakama Jinja (Karakama Shrine) is an ancient shrine located in the mountains at 408 Karakawa-cho, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture. According to the guides from Izumo City and the Shimane Prefecture Association of Shrines, the shrine appears as "Karakichi-sha" in the Izumo no Kuni Fudoki (733 CE), in the section on Izumo District, and also in the Engishiki Jinmyocho (927 CE) as "Karakama Jinja" — an ancient shrine of long provenance, with Susanoo no Mikoto (Takehaya Susanoo-no-Mikoto) as its deity. There is a tradition of an "ama-fune" (celestial boat) in the rear precinct that Susanoo no Mikoto is said to have ridden when crossing from Silla (ancient Korea) by ship, along with traditions connecting the shrine to iron and furnace (kama) culture of Korean Peninsula origin. The shrine name "Karakama" means "the furnace (kama) of Kara (ancient Korea)," suggesting connections with iron-smelting and iron culture from the Korean Peninsula. The approach path is known for its formidable structure — approximately 300 stone steps from the base to the main hall, and a massive rock gap approximately 45 centimeters wide immediately before the main hall that must be passed through for worship. The path is not paved and mobile phone signals are virtually absent in this mountain shrine. The shrine is introduced in official materials from Izumo City and the Shimane Prefecture Association of Shrines as one of the representative ancient shrines of Shimane, and is known as a unique ancient shrine offering a distinctive worship experience — combining a difficult mountain approach, a rock-gap passage, Susanoo no Mikoto veneration, the Silla arrival tradition, and iron culture — very different from the mainstream large-scale, well-developed shrine visits at Izumo Taisha. [Izumo City: Karakama Jinja](https://www.city.izumo.shimane.jp/www/contents/1610698269433/index.html), [Shimane Prefecture Association of Shrines: Karakama Jinja](https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/izumo/a8887760bc82186da6b7257897e32284bf08cbd7.html)
deepdive.cultural_context_jp_en: The Izumo region has, since ancient times, been a land of mythology recorded in the Izumo no Kuni Fudoki, Kojiki, and Nihon Shoki, with myths and traditions concerning Susanoo no Mikoto, Okuninushi no Mikoto, and Amaterasu Omikami concentrated, and many ancient shrines surviving including Izumo Taisha, Susa Jinja, Yaegaki Jinja, and Kamosu Jinja. Among these, Karakama Jinja is a shrine in which the Silla arrival tradition of Susanoo no Mikoto and the connection to iron culture are particularly strongly transmitted, and as its name "Karakama" indicates, connections with iron-smelting, iron culture, and furnace (kama) veneration of Korean Peninsula origin have been noted since antiquity. Ancient Izumo produced fine iron sand in the mountain backbone of the Chugoku region and prospered as the center of tatara iron-smelting culture until the early modern period, and iron-smelting, iron implements, and furnaces (kama) formed the foundation of Izumo culture. The shrine's name, deity, and traditions are treated as a research subject in mythology studies, ancient history, the history of iron-smelting, and religious sociology as a shrine that transmits the memory of Izumo's iron culture and cultural exchange with the Korean Peninsula / Silla. Also, the form of worship in which one passes through a rock gap before the main hall is connected to iwakura (sacred rock), iwato (rock cave) veneration that deifies topographic features such as rock outcrops, caves, and gaps as sacred, and is positioned as a rare worship form in which passing through the rock gap symbolically marks the transition from the everyday world to the sacred realm. In contrast to the large-scale, well-developed mainstream Izumo Taisha worship, it has recently been attracting attention among pilgrims interested in mythology, ancient history, and shrine culture as a unique ancient shrine with high distinctiveness combining a difficult mountain approach, rock-gap passage, Susanoo no Mikoto veneration, Silla arrival tradition, and iron culture.
deepdive.local_perspective_jp_en: Karakawa-cho, Izumo City, Shimane Prefecture, is a mountain settlement in the central to slightly northern part of Izumo City, which prospered through tatara iron-smelting, forestry, and agriculture until the early modern period. Karakama Jinja has been transmitted across generations by local Karakawa-cho residents, ujiko (parishioners), and the district community as the ancient shrine and ujigami (tutelary deity) of the area, forming the district's identity. The maintenance of the approach path, preservation of the main hall, and advancement of festival rites have been inherited by ujiko, district attendants, and shrine priests in a division of roles across generations. While the shrine accepts free visits from tourists and pilgrims, this is a shrine in a mountain valley with a formidable approach including a difficult mountain path, approximately 300 stone steps, and a rock-gap passage, combined with Susanoo no Mikoto veneration, the Silla arrival tradition, and iron culture; pilgrims are strongly expected to ensure safety on mountain paths and rocky terrain, to be considerate of the approach path and main hall, and to approach the district community, ujiko, and shrine priests with a posture of respectful worship. Positioned in a context different from mainstream Izumo Taisha worship, mocking, prurient, or entertainment-seeking worship, photography, and social media sharing are to be refrained from, and an attitude of respectful worship with understanding of the mythology, ancient history, and shrine provenance, and respect for the district community's transmission efforts, is required. [Izumo City: Karakama Jinja](https://www.city.izumo.shimane.jp/www/contents/1610698269433/index.html), [Shimane Prefecture Association of Shrines: Karakama Jinja](https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/izumo/a8887760bc82186da6b7257897e32284bf08cbd7.html)
